summary: I enlisted in the Army just before the Korean War started, and I was sent overseas around 1951. I was assigned to the 3rd Infantry Division and saw combat mostly in late 1951 and through most of 1952. The war was tough, with lots of patrols and artillery fire, and I experienced the rotation system where units would take turns on the front lines. I was lucky not to be injured during my time there, and I wrote letters home regularly to keep my family updated.

We shipped out from San Francisco when we were sent overseas to the Korean War. It was the port where our journey began before we docked in Yokohama, Japan. Leaving from San Francisco marked the start of a big change for me, heading into a war zone far from home.

After leaving San Francisco, we docked at Yokohama, Japan, where we stayed for about a week to process before heading on to Korea. It was a brief stop but an important one to get ready for what was ahead. Yokohama was like a gateway between the States and the Korean front.

When I arrived in Korea, I was assigned to the 3rd Infantry Division's 7th Regiment and sent to the front lines near the 38th parallel. I experienced combat there from late 1951 through most of 1952, doing patrols and firing missions as a lead scout and later as a bazooka man. Korea was a harsh place, with constant danger and the need to stay alert, but I was fortunate to come through without injury.

I was assigned to the 3rd Infantry Division's 7th Regiment when I got to Korea. My roles included being the lead scout and later the bazooka man for my squad. The division was stationed mostly around the 38th parallel, where we held positions and engaged in patrols and firefights. I got along well with my squad leader and fellow soldiers during my time there.

During my time in Korea, we were often exchanging fire with the Chinese People's Volunteer Army across the 38th parallel. They were the enemy forces we faced, and their mortar shells would come down near us when we engaged enemy bunkers. The fighting was tense, but both sides were cautious, trying not to expose themselves unnecessarily.

Our unit was stationed along the 38th parallel north, which was the dividing line between North and South Korea during the war. We held positions there and exchanged fire with enemy forces, including the Chinese. It was a tough spot because after the initial push to the Chinese border, we had to withdraw back to this line, which was a bit of a morale buster for many of us.
I didn't mention Seattle in my story, so I don't have any personal experiences related to that place during my service in the Korean War.
Camp Carson wasn't part of my story or experience during the Korean War, so I don't have any memories or details about it from my time in the Army.
